subject: microbiology-ii
title: Microbiology II (MD1043) — High-Yield Notes
description: Condensed revision notes across 19 topics: virology, mycology, parasitology and laboratory diagnosis.

sections.title: General Virology — structure, genome, replication ⭐
sections.html:
What a virus is

  • Obligate intracellular parasites — no ribosomes, no mitochondria, no cytoplasm, no nucleus; cannot make their own energy or proteins. Reproduce by assembly of parts (never binary fission).
  • Only one nucleic acid: DNA or RNA, never both. Visible only by electron microscopy.
  • Considered "alive" only because they produce similar descendants & mutate.

Virion components

  • Capsid = protein shell (capsomers). The part mainly made of protein ⭐ (recurrent exam answer). Protects genome, gives shape, binds receptors, is antigenic.
  • Envelope = host membrane + viral glycoprotein spikes. Enveloped viruses are labile (heat, acid, lipid solvents/detergents). "Naked" = no envelope.
  • Serotype = subcategory by surface antigen. Measles = 1 serotype; Polio = 3; Rhinovirus = ~100–160 ⭐. Many serotypes → immune evasion, no lasting immunity.

Genome & the big classification table

ClassKey families / viruses
dsDNAPapilloma (circular, N), Polyoma (BK/JC), Adeno (N), Herpes (E), Pox (E)
ssDNAParvovirus B19 (very small, naked)
dsRNAReoviridae → Rotavirus (segmented, naked)
(+)ssRNAPicorna (Polio, HAV, Entero, Rhino), Calici (Noro), Flavi (YF, HCV, Dengue, WNV), Toga (Rubella, Chikungunya), Corona
(−)ssRNAOrthomyxo (Influenza, segmented), Arena (Lassa), Bunya (Hanta/CCHF/Californ.), Paramyxo (Measles, RSV, mumps, paraflu), Rhabdo (Rabies), Filo (Ebola/Marburg)
(+)ssRNA-RTRetro → HIV, HTLV
dsDNA-RTHepadna → HBV (partially circular)
  • (+)ssRNA acts directly as mRNA (infectious alone). (−)ssRNA must carry its own polymerase (not infectious alone).
  • Reverse transcriptase: HIV, HTLV (retro) & HBV (via RNA intermediate) ⭐. HIV also has integrase + protease.

Replication steps

  • Attachment → penetration → uncoating (→ eclipse phase: no infectious virus inside) → mRNA/protein synthesis → genome replication → assembly → release.
  • Naked viruses released by cell lysis; enveloped released by budding (can shed without killing the cell → persistent infection).

Genetic variation ⭐

  • Antigenic drift = random point mutations (small change) → epidemics/outbreaks. RNA polymerases lack proofreading → high mutation rate.
  • Antigenic shift = reassortment of segmented genomes (Influenza, Rota) or recombination → new strain → pandemics.

Cultivation

  • Viruses are grown in living substrates: cell cultures (CPE), embryonated eggs, animals — never on cell-free/agar media.
  • HPV cannot be cultured in vitro. Diagnosis of most viruses no longer relies on culture (molecular/PCR now first line).

sections.title: Viral pathogenicity, tropism & persistence
sections.html:
Tropism

  • Determined by viral attachment protein ↔ host receptor. A virus establishes in specific tissues of specific animal species ⭐ (not "any tissue of any species").
  • Examples: SARS-CoV-2 Spike ↔ ACE2; Influenza haemagglutinin ↔ sialic acid (HA site 226: Leu→α-2,6 human, Gln→α-2,3 avian); HIV gp120 ↔ CD4 + CCR5/CXCR4; EBV/CMV ↔ CD21.

Infection outcomes

  • Lytic: cell death + progeny + disease.
  • Persistent/chronic: cell survives, progeny produced, ± antigenic transformation.
  • Latent: genome stays, no progeny (HSV, VZV, EBV, CMV).
  • Abortive: defective virus / non-permissive cell.

Cytopathic effects

  • Cell fusion → multinucleated giant cells / syncytia (paramyxo: measles, RSV, paraflu; HSV, VZV).
  • Inclusion bodies: Negri bodies (rabies), owl's-eye (CMV), Cowdry A (HSV/VZV), koilocytes (HPV).

Carcinogenesis (see Oncogenic section)

  • Virus supplies/activates growth genes, inactivates tumour-suppressors, or blocks apoptosis — via integration into host DNA.

sections.title: Herpesviruses (HSV, VZV, EBV, CMV, HHV6/7/8) ⭐
sections.html:
Family basics

  • All dsDNA, icosahedral, enveloped (from nuclear membrane); establish latency.
  • α: HSV1/2, VZV — latency in sensory ganglia, fast growth.
  • β: CMV, HHV6, HHV7 — slow, multi-located.
  • γ: EBV, HHV8 — grow in lymphoblasts.

HSV-1 / HSV-2

  • HSV-1 latency in trigeminal ganglion (labial herpes, gingivostomatitis, encephalitis — temporal lobe, ~70% mortality untreated). HSV-2 latency in sacral ganglia (genital herpes, meningitis).
  • Neonatal herpes: from genital HSV at birth, often during asymptomatic recurrence — high mortality.
  • Dendritic keratitis → scarring → blindness. Rx: acyclovir (activated by viral thymidine kinase).

VZV (HHV-3)

  • Primary = chickenpox (lesions in all stages simultaneously — vs smallpox same stage). Reactivation = herpes zoster/shingles (dermatomal, post-herpetic neuralgia).
  • A non-immune person exposed to shingles → develops chickenpox ⭐. Can cause pneumonia + encephalitis.
  • Vaccines: varicella = live attenuated; zoster = recombinant gE subunit (>60 y).

EBV (HHV-4)

  • Infects B-lymphocytes via CD21; latency as episome. Infectious mononucleosis: fever, pharyngitis, lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly.
  • Findings: atypical lymphocytes (early), heterophile (Paul-Bunnell) antibodies, Monospot, VCA-IgM. Note: lymphocytosis, not decrease ⭐.
  • Neoplasia ⭐: Burkitt lymphoma (c-myc t8;14, + malaria cofactor in Africa), nasopharyngeal carcinoma (SE Asia), Hodgkin, gastric ca, post-transplant lymphoma; linked to MS.

CMV (HHV-5)

  • Most common congenital infection ⭐ ("owl's eye" intranuclear inclusions). Microcephaly, chorioretinitis, deafness, petechiae, hepatosplenomegaly.
  • The only congenital infection treatable before & after birth. Serious in transplant/AIDS. Rx: ganciclovir, foscarnet. Transmitted many routes incl. sexual.

HHV-6/7 = roseola (6th disease); HHV-8 = Kaposi sarcoma.

Main viral causes of congenital infection ⭐

  • Rubella, CMV, Parvo B19, HIV, HSV, VZV, HBV/HCV, enteroviruses. (Toxoplasma is the non-viral classic.)

sections.title: Hepatitis viruses (A, B, C, D, E) + HBV serology ⭐
sections.html:
Route & chronicity

VirusGenomeRouteChronic?Vaccine
HAV(+)ssRNA picorna, nakedFecal-oralNoKilled
HEV(+)ssRNA, nakedFecal-oral (pigs, zoonosis)Only if immunocompromised(regional)
HBVdsDNA-RT, envelopedBlood/sexual/verticalYesRecombinant HBsAg
HCV(+)ssRNA flavi, envelopedBlood (less sexual)Yes (~50–80%)None
HDVdefective RNANeeds HBV (borrows HBsAg)Yesvia HBV vaccine
  • HAV mainly via the GI tract ⭐. Not all hepatitis viruses share DNA / chronicity / vaccine → "none of the answers correct" ⭐.
  • Liver damage in HAV/HBV is immune-mediated (not direct cytopathic).

HBV serology ⭐⭐ (very high-yield)

MarkerMeaning
HBsAgActive infection / infectivity (>6 months = chronic carrier)
Anti-HBsImmunity — after resolved infection or vaccination
Anti-HBc IgMAcute infection (also window period)
Anti-HBc IgG (total)Past exposure (present in resolved + chronic)
HBeAgHigh infectivity / active replication
Anti-HBeLower infectivity
  • Recovery + immunity ⭐ = Anti-HBs (+) & Anti-HBc (+), HBsAg (−).
  • Vaccination only = Anti-HBs (+) alone (anti-HBc negative).
  • Chronic HBV = HBsAg (+) >6 months, Anti-HBc (+), Anti-HBs (−).
  • Acute = HBsAg (+) + Anti-HBc IgM (+).

HBV notes

  • cccDNA = persistence/reactivation reservoir. Perinatal infection → 90–95% become chronic carriers (age effect).
  • Rx: PEG-IFN or nucleos(t)ides (tenofovir, entecavir). HCC risk ↑ (integration, c-myc). HCV is now curable with DAAs (SVR >90%); HBV = suppression, HIV = lifelong.

sections.title: Respiratory viruses (Influenza, Paraflu, RSV, Corona, Rhino, Adeno) ⭐
sections.html:
Influenza (Orthomyxo, (−)ssRNA, 8 segments, enveloped)

  • Surface Haemagglutinin (attach, sialic acid) + Neuraminidase (release). Grown in embryonated eggs/MDCK.
  • A = frequent shifts → pandemics (most likely to cause a pandemic ⭐); B = outbreaks; C = sporadic.
  • Drift (mutation → epidemics) vs shift (reassortment → pandemic). Vaccine changes yearly (A/H1N1, A/H3N2, B).
  • Flu shot = annually ⭐. Injectable = inactivated/subunit; intranasal = live attenuated (cold-adapted, IgA).
  • Rx: M2 blockers (amantadine/rimantadine, type A only), neuraminidase inhibitors (oseltamivir/zanamivir/peramivir), baloxavir. Complication: post-influenza bacterial pneumonia (S. pneumoniae, S. aureus, H. influenzae).

Paramyxoviruses (Paraflu, RSV, measles, mumps) — (−)ssRNA, enveloped, F protein → syncytia

  • Parainfluenza → croup (laryngotracheobronchitis, "seal-bark" cough).
  • RSV → bronchiolitis & pneumonia in infants; most common LRTI/hospitalisation cause in infants; serious in elderly/immunocompromised. No lasting immunity. Rx supportive ± ribavirin; passive mAb (nirsevimab) & maternal/older-adult vaccine (pre-F).
  • Respiratory paramyxos → no viraemia; measles & mumps → systemic viraemia.

Coronaviruses ((+)ssRNA, enveloped, longest RNA genome)

  • 2nd most common cause of common cold; SARS (bats, ~9%), MERS (camels, ~34%), SARS-CoV-2 (ACE2). Rx: nirmatrelvir/ritonavir (Paxlovid, protease inh.), molnupiravir, remdesivir.
  • Corona is NOT a myxovirus ⭐ (recurring "which is not a myxovirus/paramyxovirus" → Corona).

Rhinovirus & Adenovirus

  • Rhinovirus: picorna, ~100–160 serotypes → common cold; short, type-specific, ineffective immunity ⭐; no vaccine, no specific antiviral (too many serotypes).
  • Adenovirus: dsDNA naked, ~41 types → pharyngitis, pneumonia, conjunctivitis "pink eye", haemorrhagic cystitis ⭐, gastroenteritis (types 40/41).

sections.title: Viral gastroenteritis (Rota, Noro, Adeno, Astro, Sapo) ⭐
sections.html:
• Viruses cause ~70% of infectious gastroenteritis. Mechanism = infect small-bowel enterocytes, villous injury, malabsorption + secretion → watery, non-inflammatory diarrhoea (vs invasive/toxin-mediated bacteria → bloody/inflammatory).
  • Rotavirus ⭐: dsRNA reovirus (wheel-shaped, segmented). Most important severe paediatric gastroenteritis (<5 y), winter, low infectious dose. NSP4 = viral enterotoxin. Has a live-attenuated oral vaccine (Rotarix, RotaTeq) ⭐ — 1998 Rotashield withdrawn (intussusception).
  • Norovirus (Calicivirus, naked ssRNA): leading cause of outbreaks in adults (cruise ships, hospitals), "winter vomiting bug", very low dose, resistant to alcohol gel, vomiting prominent, no vaccine.
  • Enteric adenovirus (40/41), astrovirus, sapovirus = milder/childhood.
  • Enteroviruses are NOT typical GI pathogens (they cause meningitis, HFMD, myocarditis, rash).
  • Amoeba (Entamoeba histolytica) also causes diarrhoea → "which causes diarrhoea? all of the above" ⭐. Treatment = rehydration (ORS/IV); antivirals not used.

sections.title: Measles · Mumps · Rubella ⭐
sections.html:
Measles (Paramyxo, Morbillivirus, (−)ssRNA)

  • ONE serotype ⭐ ("many serotypes" is the false statement). Highly contagious (droplet), 90% of non-immune infected.
  • Clinical: 3 C's (Cough, Coryza, Conjunctivitis) + fever, then maculopapular rash (face→trunk). Koplik spots = pathognomonic ("grains of salt, red halo," buccal mucosa).
  • Complications: giant-cell (interstitial) pneumonia, post-infectious encephalomyelitis, SSPE (subacute sclerosing panencephalitis, defective virus lacking M protein, years later). Severe if malnourished/vit-A deficient/immunosuppressed.
  • Confers lifelong immunity. Vaccine = live attenuated (MMR), 2 doses; contraindicated in pregnancy/immunosuppression.

Mumps

  • Paramyxo. Parotitis; complications: orchitis → sterility in men ⭐, aseptic meningitis, pancreatitis. Prevented by MMR ⭐ ("which is an RNA virus → mumps").

Rubella (Togavirus, (+)ssRNA)

  • Mild in adults but congenital rubella syndrome: cataracts, deafness, congenital heart disease.
  • Severity is inversely proportional to gestational age — worst in 1st trimester ⭐. Not classically sexually transmitted ⭐. Prevented by MMR.

sections.title: Oncogenic viruses & HPV ⭐
sections.html:
• ~20% of cancers worldwide are viral. Mechanisms: (1) supply/activate growth oncogenes, (2) inactivate tumour-suppressors (p53, Rb), (3) block apoptosis — via integration.
  • HPV (dsDNA, circular, naked; cannot be cultured): high-risk 16, 18 → cervical/anogenital/oropharyngeal cancer. E6→degrades p53, E7→degrades Rb (p105). Dx: Pap (koilocytes), HPV-DNA/PCR. Vaccine = recombinant subunit (bivalent 70%, quadrivalent 90%, 9-valent >90%); both sexes.
  • HBV → HCC (integration, c-myc). HCV → HCC (NS5A sequesters p53 + chronic inflammation).
  • EBV → Burkitt (c-myc t8;14), nasopharyngeal ca, Hodgkin. HHV-8 → Kaposi sarcoma (c-kit).
  • HTLV-1 (retrovirus) → Adult T-cell leukemia/lymphoma (tax gene); + HAM/TSP myelopathy. Transmission like HIV (blood, sexual, breastfeeding).
  • Not oncogenic ⭐: CMV (in the 2020-style "which virus is NOT oncogenic" the oncogenic set is HPV, HCV, HTLV, EBV, HBV, HHV8 → the odd one out is CMV).
  • DNA oncovirus: integration → no new virions. RNA oncovirus (retro): uses reverse transcriptase, may still produce virions.

sections.title: CNS viruses (Entero/Polio, Rabies, arboviral encephalitis)
sections.html:
• Meningitis = meninges; encephalitis = brain. Viral CSF: clear, lymphocytes ↑, protein mild ↑, glucose normal. IgM in CSF = CNS infection (IgM doesn't cross BBB).
  • Enteroviruses (Coxsackie, ECHO, polio) = most common cause of aseptic meningitis; summer/autumn; faecal-oral; RT-PCR.
  • Poliovirus: mostly asymptomatic, can cause aseptic meningitis + flaccid paralysis; faecal-oral; best sample = stool ⭐. Vaccines: Sabin (OPV, live, oral, IgA) / Salk (IPV, inactivated, injected).
  • HSV-1 = leading cause of sporadic encephalitis (temporal lobe, 70% mortality untreated → acyclovir). HSV-2 = meningitis.
  • Rabies (Rhabdovirus, bullet-shaped, (−)ssRNA): animal bite (mammals — dogs, bats — not birds); travels to brain; incubation 20–40 d; once symptomatic → nearly 100% fatal. Negri bodies. Prevented by vaccinating animals + post-exposure prophylaxis ⭐.
  • West Nile (Flavivirus): Culex mosquito, birds reservoir; 80% asymptomatic, <1% neuroinvasive (~10% mortality). Endemic in Greece since 2010.
  • JC virus (polyoma) → PML in immunosuppressed/AIDS.

sections.title: Arboviruses & viral haemorrhagic fevers (Toga/Flavi/Arena/Bunya/Filo)
sections.html:
• Arboviruses = arthropod-borne (mosquito/tick/sandfly). Roboviruses = rodent-borne. All enveloped RNA, geographically restricted to their host/vector.
  • Haemorrhagic fever = increased vascular permeability from uncontrolled cytokines → bleeding + shock; leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, ↑liver enzymes. Public-health concern (person-to-person spread, high mortality).
  • Togaviridae ((+)ssRNA): Alphavirus (Chikungunya — Aedes, severe polyarthritis; Sindbis) + Rubivirus (rubella).
  • Flaviviridae ((+)ssRNA): Dengue (Aedes, 4 serotypes, severe dengue on heterotypic reinfection — antibody-dependent enhancement), Yellow fever (vaccine exists ⭐), West Nile, Usutu, TBE (Ixodes tick / unpasteurised milk; vaccine exists), HCV.
  • Bunyavirales ((−)ssRNA, segmented S/M/L): Hantavirus (rodents, inhaled excreta → HFRS renal / HPS pulmonary), Nairovirus = CCHF (Hyalomma tick + person-to-person, 30–50% mortality, ribavirin; cases in Greece), Phlebovirus (sandfly: Toscana, Sandfly fever; RVF by mosquito).
  • Filoviridae ((−)ssRNA, filamentous): Ebola (fruit-bat reservoir, contact with body fluids, up to 90% mortality, Ervebo vaccine) & Marburg. BSL-4.
  • Arenaviridae: Lassa (rodent Mastomys; leukocytosis; ribavirin), LCMV, New-World HFs (Junin, Machupo).
  • Zoonotic/animal-transmitted set ⭐: West Nile, Rabies, Hantavirus → "all of the above."

sections.title: Parvovirus B19 ⭐
sections.html:
• Smallest virus, ssDNA, naked. Infects erythroid progenitor cells (via P antigen/globoside) ⭐ → kills red-cell precursors.
  • Diseases: erythema infectiosum / fifth disease ("slapped cheek"), arthropathy, aplastic crisis in sickle-cell/haemolytic anaemia, hydrops fetalis (congenital infection).
  • Classic exam lines ⭐: "virus that circulates and kills erythrocytes → Parvo B19"; "infects erythroid lineage / aplastic crisis in sickle cell → Parvovirus."

sections.title: HIV — biology, course & lab diagnosis ⭐
sections.html:
• Retrovirus, (+)ssRNA (2 copies) + reverse transcriptase, integrase, protease. Envelope gp120/gp41. Enters via gp120 → CD4 + co-receptor CCR5 (macrophage/M-tropic) or CXCR4 (T-tropic). People lacking CCR5 are resistant ⭐.
  • Replication: RNA→DNA (RT, error-prone → quasispecies) → integrase → provirus → latent reservoir (GALT, lymph nodes). Downregulates MHC-I → evades CD8.
  • CD4 count = best marker of progression; CD4/CD8 ratio monitored ⭐. Transmission via blood/semen/vaginal fluid/breast milk — NOT mosquitoes ⭐.
  • Rx = AZT (azidothymidine) & combination ART ⭐; combination prevents resistance; also PrEP/PEP. Does not clear the latent reservoir.
  • Diagnosis / window period ⭐:
    • HIV RNA ~day 10 (earliest; not for routine screening).
    • p24 antigen ~day 4–10 (transient) — found in blood for early definitive diagnosis ⭐.
    • IgM then IgG antibodies later.
    • 4th-generation test detects HIV antibodies + p24 antigen ⭐. Confirm with Western blot (2 of gp24/gp41/gp120/160) or a 2nd 4th-gen assay.
    • Neonate: maternal IgG persists → use HIV DNA PCR (gold standard in infancy).
  • High sensitivity = screening (few false negatives); high specificity = confirmation (few false positives).

sections.title: Antiviral drugs ⭐
sections.html:
DrugTarget / mechanismUse
Acyclovir (valacyclovir, famciclovir)Guanosine analogue, activated by viral thymidine kinase, inhibits viral DNA polymeraseHSV, VZV (crystalluria/nephrotoxicity)
GanciclovirActivated by CMV UL97CMV (bone-marrow suppression)
FoscarnetDirect DNA-pol inhibitor, no phosphorylation neededResistant HSV/CMV (nephrotoxic)
CidofovirNucleotide analogueCMV retinitis, resistant HSV, adeno
Oseltamivir/zanamivir/peramivirNeuraminidase inhibitorInfluenza A & B (start <48 h)
Amantadine/rimantadineM2 ion-channel blockerInfluenza A only
AZT / ARTRT/integrase/protease inhibitors (combination)HIV
Tenofovir, entecavirNucleos(t)ide, inhibit HBV polymerase/RTChronic HBV (suppression)
Sofosbuvir + other DAAsTarget viral replication proteinsHCV (curative, SVR >90%)
Nirmatrelvir/ritonavir; remdesivirProtease inh.; RNA-pol inh.COVID-19
  • Nucleotide analogs = a category of antiviral drug ⭐. Acyclovir's low toxicity = viral-TK activation + higher affinity for viral polymerase.
  • Resistance mainly from viral mutation: acyclovir → TK mutation; HIV → RT mutation; influenza → neuraminidase mutation.
  • Specific antivirals exist for: HSV/VZV, CMV, influenza, HIV, HBV, HCV, COVID. No specific antiviral: rhinovirus, most gastro viruses.

sections.title: Viral vaccines ⭐
sections.html:
TypeExamples
Live attenuatedMMR (measles/mumps/rubella), Varicella (VZV), OPV (Sabin polio), Rotavirus (oral), Yellow fever, intranasal flu
Inactivated / killedHepatitis A, IPV (Salk polio), rabies, injectable flu
Subunit / recombinantHBV (HBsAg in yeast), HPV, zoster gE, RSV pre-F, protein-based COVID
Viral-vector / mRNACOVID (adenovirus vector; S-protein mRNA)
  • Injectable live-attenuated ⭐ = measles (flu injectable = inactivated; HPV/HBV = recombinant).
  • Live: usually 1 dose, long immunity, cellular + IgA, but thermolabile & contraindicated in immunocompromised/pregnancy. Inactivated: ≥1 dose, adjuvant, shorter IgG-only immunity, safer.
  • No vaccine exists for ⭐: CMV, HCV, HIV, rhinovirus, parainfluenza, EBV, HSV, Dengue-none... (Dengue & Ebola & TBE & YF do have vaccines). Classic "which has a vaccine? HSV / VZV / HCV / EBV → VZV."
  • Smallpox eradicated (1977); polio near-eradicated; measles/mumps/rubella greatly reduced.

sections.title: Immunology of viral infection
sections.html:
• Extracellular virus → antibodies, complement, phagocytes. Intracellular → MHC-I → CD8 CTL, NK cells ("missing self").
  • Type I interferons (α/β): induced by dsRNA/viruses; induce antiviral state in neighbouring cells (degrade mRNA, block protein synthesis), ↑MHC-I, activate NK. Host-species specific; cause flu-like symptoms. Type II = IFN-γ (macrophage activator).
  • NK cells: kill cells with low MHC-I (which CTLs miss); major IFN-γ source; enhanced by IL-12.
  • Viral symptoms are largely immune-mediated: IL-1/IL-6/TNF-α → fever; IFN → malaise/myalgia.
  • Neutralizing antibodies block attachment/entry; immunity is serotype-specific. Mucosal IgA (e.g. from OPV) blocks at surfaces.
  • Liver damage in HBV/HCV = host CTL/NK killing infected hepatocytes (not the virus directly).
  • Immune evasion: antigenic shift/drift, MHC-I downregulation, decoy cytokine mimics (EBV IL-10 homolog; poxvirus cytokine binders).

sections.title: Infections in the immunocompromised
sections.html:
• Phagocyte/complement/antibody defects → extracellular organisms: bacteria & fungi (Candida, Aspergillus), encapsulated bugs after splenectomy.
  • T-cell defects → intracellular: viruses (CMV, HSV, EBV, adeno, JC/BK), fungi (Candida, Aspergillus, Histoplasma, Cryptococcus, Pneumocystis), protozoa (Toxoplasma, Leishmania, Cryptosporidium), Strongyloides.
  • Neutropenia (ANC <500) → neutropenic fever = emergency (antibiotics <60 min). Biofilm on lines/prostheses (S. epidermidis).
  • Transplant timeline: early = bacterial (lines/mucosal breach); 1–6 mo = opportunistic (CMV, fungi, PCP, mycobacteria); reactivation of latent HBV/HCV/CMV.

sections.title: Mycology — yeasts, moulds, dimorphic, dermatophytes + antifungals ⭐
sections.html:
Fungal basics

  • Eukaryotes; cell wall of glucans + chitin; membrane uses ergosterol (drug target). Heterotrophs.
  • Yeasts = unicellular, bud/fission, moist/pasty colonies (Candida, Cryptococcus). Moulds = multicellular hyphae, spores, fluffy colonies (Aspergillus, Mucor). "Hyphae → moulds" ⭐.
  • Yeasts (Candida) are part of normal flora & relate to daily diet/living (bread/beer) ⭐.

Key pathogens

  • Candida (esp. C. albicans; C. auris = MDR): commonest nosocomial fungal infection; most invasive candidiasis arises endogenously from GI/mucosa; risk = central lines, broad-spectrum antibiotics, neutropenia, diabetes. Candida in blood culture is never a contaminant. Germ-tube test.
  • Cryptococcus neoformans: encapsulated yeast (soil, pigeon droppings); inhaled → meningitis/meningoencephalitis in T-cell-defective (AIDS). India ink / CrAg. Neurotropic (uses host catecholamines for melanin).
  • Aspergillus (A. fumigatus): septate acute-angle hyphae, angioinvasion; allergic (ABPA) ⭐, chronic, invasive (neutropenia). Galactomannan antigen. Common airborne contaminant → positive sputum ≠ infection unless risk factors.
  • Mucorales (Rhizopus/Mucor) "black fungus": broad, non-septate ribbon-like hyphae; rhinocerebral in diabetic ketoacidosis; angioinvasive, mortality 70–100%.
  • Dimorphic (mould at 25°C, yeast at 37°C): Histoplasma capsulatum (systemic mycosis ⭐, bird/bat droppings), Blastomyces, Coccidioides, Paracoccidioides — always pathogenic (not colonisers).
  • Dermatophytes (Trichophyton, Microsporum, Epidermophyton): keratinophilic → tinea/ringworm, onychomycosis. Sabouraud agar, KOH, Wood's lamp. (Malassezia furfur → pityriasis versicolor — not Candida ⭐.)
  • Pneumocystis jirovecii: reclassified as fungus; PCP in AIDS.

Antifungals ⭐

  • Amphotericin B / nystatin = polyenes: bind ergosterol → disrupt the fungal cell MEMBRANE, fungicidal ⭐ (not cell wall, not fungistatic, not azole). Amphotericin B ↔ ergosterol.
  • Azoles (fluconazole, itraconazole): inhibit ergosterol synthesis (14-α-demethylase). C. glabrata/krusei resist fluconazole.
  • Echinocandins: inhibit β-glucan synthesis (cell wall).

sections.title: Parasitology — protozoa & helminths (exam-critical) ⭐
sections.html:
No dedicated parasite deck was in your files, but past papers & recalls test these heavily — here's the exam-focused set.

Protozoa

  • Plasmodium (malaria): intracellular (hepatocytes → RBCs) ⭐; Anopheles mosquito. Prevention today = vector/insect control (no routine vaccine historically) ⭐. Parasite of red cells, not white cells ⭐.
  • Toxoplasma gondii: intracellular; cats definitive host (transmitted by cats / undercooked meat) ⭐; congenital (chorioretinitis, hydrocephalus, calcifications); latent cysts persist for life ⭐; reactivation in AIDS (brain).
  • Trichomonas vaginalis: flagellate, sexually transmitted vaginitis; diagnosed on vaginal smear / wet-mount trophozoites ⭐; Rx metronidazole. NOT a cause of haemorrhagic diarrhoea ⭐. Extracellular (luminal).
  • Giardia lamblia: flagellate; watery diarrhoea/malabsorption; luminal (extracellular).
  • Entamoeba histolytica: bloody/amoebic dysentery + liver abscess; causes diarrhoea.
  • Leishmania: intramacrophage; transmitted by sandfly (NOT mosquito ⭐); cutaneous/visceral (kala-azar). Not an intestinal parasite ⭐.
  • Cryptosporidium: diarrhoea in AIDS.

Helminths — multicellular parasites ⭐

  • Nematodes (roundworms): Ascaris lumbricoides (Löffler eosinophilic pneumonia), Enterobius vermicularis (pinworm/oxyuris) → perianal itching in children ⭐ (Scotch-tape test), Strongyloides, Onchocerca (river blindness), Loa loa (eye).
  • Cestodes (tapeworms): Echinococcus granulosus → hydatid cyst (liver/lung) ⭐ = the intestinal cestode in "which is a tapeworm"; Taenia (cysticercosis ⭐).
  • Helminth infection → peripheral eosinophilia ⭐.

Recurring parasitology answers

  • Not intestinal parasite → Leishmania. Vaginal-smear diagnosis → Trichomonas. Intracellular → Plasmodium/Toxoplasma. Transmitted by cats → Toxoplasma. Perianal in children → Enterobius. Tapeworm → Echinococcus/Taenia. Cysticercosis = parasite (Taenia). Eosinophilia → helminths. Fungi/protozoa moving by flagella statement is often the wrong option (not all do).

sections.title: Laboratory diagnosis (viral · fungal · syndromic)
sections.html:
• Virus isolation: cell cultures (look for CPE), embryonated eggs, animals — living substrates only. Haemagglutination/haemadsorption for influenza/paraflu.
  • Molecular (PCR / RT-PCR) = method of choice now for most respiratory/CNS/GI viruses & COVID; multiplex "syndromic" panels detect co-infections (but detection ≠ disease; can't tell live from dead).
  • Serology (ELISA, IFA, CLIA): IgM = recent/acute; 4× IgG rise = infection; total Ab = immunity. Cross-reactivity among flaviviruses.
  • Viral markers ⭐: Monospot/heterophile (EBV), p24 (HIV), Negri bodies (rabies), owl's eye (CMV) — VDRL is NOT viral (syphilis) ⭐. Chronic hepatitis indicated by HBsAg >6 mo / HBeAg / HBV DNA.
  • Fungal lab: KOH + calcofluor/India ink microscopy, Sabouraud culture (25–30°C, up to 4 wks), MALDI-TOF; antigens: Cryptococcus CrAg, Aspergillus galactomannan, pan-fungal β-D-glucan (BDG) (negative for Cryptococcus & Mucor). Histoplasma culture = biohazard (dimorphic).
  • PCR steps ⭐: denaturation ~95°C, annealing ~50–60°C, extension ~72°C.
